verification.note:
Verified against /Users/user/git/cc-goodies/openspec/changes/audit-plugin-best-practices/rubric.json. HOOK-8 rule: "Set an explicit timeout per hook appropriate to its work (command default 60s... recommended 5-600s, must be numeric)..."; appliesTo: "plugins that ship hooks"; NO conflictNote field exists. The item applies — voice-notify ships Notification + Stop hooks.

Evidence accurate: /Users/user/git/cc-goodies/plugins/voice-notify/.claude-plugin/plugin.json lines 13-21 (Notification) and 26-36 (Stop) declare no "timeout" field. Confirmed by reading the file.

Real gap, not n-a and not a documented house choice: there is no conflictNote on HOOK-8, and CLAUDE.md only documents "async": true usage — it says nothing exempting async hooks from declaring a timeout. Decisively, every sibling hook plugin in this same marketplace DOES set an explicit timeout: git-guard=10, shell-guard=10, rtk-hook=15 (verified in their plugin.json). So the marketplace house pattern is to set timeouts; voice-notify is the lone deviation. The auditor's "async + bounded say => harmless" argument is valid impact reasoning but the rule targets declaring the field explicitly, not whether behavior breaks.

Severity correction: nit -> minor. nit is cosmetic; this is convention drift — a recommended manifest field that all three peer hook plugins set but this one omits. It does not fail `claude plugins validate`, is not a required schema field, and does not break behavior (command default 60s applies), so it does not rise to major/blocker. Convention drift = minor per the provided rubric.

Fix: add "timeout": <numeric seconds> (e.g. 5) to each hook handler in plugins/voice-notify/.claude-plugin/plugin.json, matching the sibling-plugin pattern.

verification.note:
Evidence verified and accurate. plugin.json line 9 does declare "license": "MIT" (SPDX field present), and plugins/voice-notify/ ships no LICENSE file — confirmed via ls (dir contains only .claude-plugin/, scripts/, README.md). A repo-root LICENSE exists at /Users/user/git/cc-goodies/LICENSE.

VAL-6 applies: voice-notify is distributed through the cc-goodies marketplace, so "plugins intended for distribution" matches. The rule is SHOULD-level and reads "include a LICENSE file (AND a SPDX license field)"; only the SPDX half is satisfied, so the LICENSE-file half is a genuine (if minor) omission.

This is NOT a defect that fails validation or breaks a documented contract: VAL-6 has no conflictNote, and `claude plugins validate` does not fail on a missing LICENSE (SHOULD, not MUST). It is also a deliberate, uniform house pattern — all 7 plugins (git-guard, project-scope, rtk-hook, session-finalise, shell-guard, statusline, voice-notify) declare SPDX MIT and none ships its own LICENSE; the root LICENSE covers the whole repo. Auditor's nit severity is correct: cosmetic packaging-consistency item, only relevant if per-plugin standalone distribution is intended. No .gitignore concern — the plugin generates no artifacts. Confirmed as a nit-level gap, not refuted.

verification.note:
Confirmed. Re-read /Users/user/git/cc-goodies/plugins/statusline/commands/statusline-uninstall.md:1-4 — frontmatter (description, allowed-tools) has no disable-model-invocation:true. The command is genuinely destructive/manual-only: step 3 edits ~/.claude/settings.json (jq del(.statusLine)), step 4 rm -f ~/.claude/team-statusline.sh. CMD-6 explicitly names "*-uninstall control commands" as the canonical appliesTo case, so the item applies — not n-a.

No conflictNote on CMD-6 and no house-rule in CLAUDE.md or the project Conventions section choosing to omit disable-model-invocation, so this is not a documented deliberate choice. It is, however, a project-wide pattern: NO command in any cc-goodies plugin sets the field (verified via grep -rn disable-model-invocation plugins/ → zero hits, including the other destructive *-uninstall commands and statusline-install). That makes it convention drift / documentation-level omission rather than a behavioral defect.

Severity minor is correct (kept). It does not fail claude plugins validate, does not break install⇄uninstall symmetry (the command still functions when manually invoked), and writes no un-revertable durable state without revert. The command body already enforces an ownership guard (step 2 only proceeds if .statusLine.command references team-statusline.sh) and per-step confirmations, so the missing frontmatter field is a defense-in-depth convention gap, not blocker/major.

verification.note: Refuting. The cited fact is accurate (neither statusline-install.md nor statusline-uninstall.md contains any <!-- --> HTML comments; the ownership guard and jq-merge rationale sit in the prose body), but it does NOT show a CMD-12 violation. CMD-12 (rubric.json:551-558, no conflictNote) reads: "Document non-obvious command logic, usage, requirements, and examples in HTML comments within the command file (they do not appear in Claude's prompt output)." The rule's stated rationale — "they do not appear in Claude's prompt output" — is precisely why it does NOT apply to this content. In these two files the "non-obvious logic" is not author-facing documentation that should be hidden from Claude; it IS the operative instruction set Claude must read and execute. Install step 6 ("merge with jq so every existing setting is preserved") and uninstall step 2 ("Check ownership — this is the critical guard ... remove it only if it references team-statusline.sh") are imperative directives driving the command's behavior. Moving them into HTML comments would strip them from Claude's prompt and break the command (the ownership guard would vanish, the jq-merge would not happen). CMD-12 targets supplementary docs/usage/requirements/examples meant for the human maintainer; the command body that instructs Claude is out of scope. This matches the repo's documented convention (CLAUDE.md: "Commands are markdown ... whose body instructs Claude ... reads, shows the diff, confirms, and merges with jq"). The item is misapplied here; correct verdict is n-a, not a gap. correctedSeverity left blank since there is no defect.

verifiedGaps.evidence: plugins/git-guard/.claude-plugin/plugin.json:3 declares version "0.2.0"; .claude-plugin/marketplace.json:24-26 git-guard entry declares no version. Only plugin.json sets it — no conflict.

This is technically compliant with the no-double-version guidance, but the rule also notes that omitting version in both falls back to commit SHA. verification.note: Refuted. The cited evidence is accurate but does NOT show a violation. MKT-11's rule is to AVOID setting version in BOTH plugin.json and the marketplace entry (because plugin.json silently wins). Verified: /Users/user/git/cc-goodies/plugins/git-guard/.claude-plugin/plugin.json:3 sets "version": "0.2.0"; /Users/user/git/cc-goodies/.claude-plugin/marketplace.json git-guard entry (lines 24-26) has only name + source + description — no version field. Confirmed programmatically that NO marketplace plugins[] entry carries a version. Therefore version is set in exactly one place, which is the compliant arrangement the rule endorses; the silent-win hazard the rule guards against is structurally absent. The auditor's own note concedes "no violation... re-scored as compliant in spirit... no fix needed" yet still filed it as gap/nit to flag the MAN-4 manual-bump dependency — but that is a different concern (manual version bumps) not what MKT-11 evaluates, and bumping a single source of truth is normal, not a defect. Correct verdict is pass, not gap. No severity applies.

verifiedGaps.evidence: commands/git-guard-uninstall.md:1-4 frontmatter sets no disable-model-invocation:true, despite being a destructive/irreversible removal command (rm -f of the conf at step 3).
verifiedGaps.note: CMD-6 says set disable-model-invocation:true for destructive/manual-only commands. git-guard-uninstall deletes ~/.claude/git-guard.conf and is meant to be user-invoked. It does back up first (.bak) and confirms, mitigating risk, but per the rubric a teardown command qualifies for the flag. git-guard.md (config writer, confirms before write) is more borderline. Fix: add 'disable-model-invocation: true' to git-guard-uninstall.md frontmatter. Scored minor (mitigated by explicit confirmation + backup).

verification.note:
Gap confirmed. Re-read /Users/user/git/cc-goodies/plugins/git-guard/commands/git-guard-uninstall.md:1-4 — frontmatter sets only `description` and `allowed-tools` (Bash(rm:*), Bash(cp:*), Bash(test:*), Bash(cat:*), Read); no `disable-model-invocation: true`. Evidence is accurate: step 3 (lines 20-23) does `rm -f` of ~/.claude/git-guard.conf, and this is an *-uninstall command.

CMD-6 (rubric.json:496-503) rule: "Set disable-model-invocation:true for commands that must be manually invoked, are destructive/irreversible, or require human judgment; use it sparingly." appliesTo: "manual-only / destructive / interactive slash commands (e.g. *-uninstall control commands)" — this command is the literal named example. CMD-6 has NO conflictNote, and grep across CLAUDE.md/README/CONTRIBUTING/plugin README finds zero mention of disable-model-invocation, so this is not a documented house-rule exception — it is a genuine omission, not a misapplied item.

Applies (not n-a): destructive (rm -f) + intended user-invoked teardown. Severity minor is correct: validate still passes (flag is optional), so not blocker; no durable-state-without-revert (this command IS the revert), so not major. The flag is functionally meaningful — it stops the model auto-invoking a destructive removal via the SlashCommand tool — but the command body mitigates with backup-to-.bak + explicit confirmation, and a command can't uninstall its own plugin anyway. Convention drift / frontmatter omission = minor; held at minor rather than nit because the rubric names destructive uninstall commands specifically.

Note for context (not part of this item's verdict): the same omission exists in every other uninstall/destructive command in the marketplace (rtk-hook-uninstall, shell-guard-uninstall, statusline-uninstall/install) — a marketplace-wide convention gap, not git-guard-specific.

verifiedGaps.evidence: commands/git-guard.md:1-52 and git-guard-uninstall.md:1-33 contain no HTML comments (<!-- -->); all guidance is in the prompt body.
verifiedGaps.note: CMD-12 suggests documenting non-obvious logic/usage/examples in HTML comments so they stay out of Claude's prompt. These commands embed usage in the prose body instead, which is functional but means example/usage text is sent to the model. Minor/cosmetic — the bodies are clear. Fix (optional): move maintainer notes into <!-- --> comments.

verification.note:
Evidence is factually accurate: neither /Users/user/git/cc-goodies/plugins/git-guard/commands/git-guard.md (1-52) nor git-guard-uninstall.md (1-33) contains HTML comments; all content is in the prose body. But this is not a violation of CMD-12 and not a gap.

CMD-12's rule (rubric.json:553): "Document non-obvious command logic, usage, requirements, and examples in HTML comments... (they do not appear in Claude's prompt output)." HTML comments exist to hold MAINTAINER notes that should be kept OUT of the model's prompt. The body of both git-guard commands is not maintainer documentation — it IS the executable instruction set Claude must read at runtime (read the conf, lead with guard state, offer the menu, merge-write the KEY=VALUE file, confirm). That content MUST reach the prompt; moving it into <!-- --> would hide it from Claude and break the command. The one example present (the KEY=VALUE snippet at git-guard.md:43-46) is deliberately shown to Claude so it produces the correct file format — exactly where it belongs.

There is no maintainer-only note in either file that is wrongly leaking into the prompt, so there is nothing CMD-12 asks to be moved. CMD-12 is conditional ("IF you have non-obvious maintainer notes, hide them in HTML comments"), not a mandate that every non-trivial command contain HTML comments. With no such notes here, the item does not apply. The auditor's own framing ("functional," "the bodies are clear," fix is "optional") confirms there is no defect. Verdict corrected to n-a; no severity. Refuted.

verification.note:
Evidence verified and accurate. plugin.json:9 declares "license": "MIT"; README.md:150-152 states "MIT © Volodymyr Styran"; plugins/git-guard/ contains no LICENSE file (dir holds only .claude-plugin/, commands/, scripts/, tests/, README.md). No .gitignore — correctly absent (no generated/local artifacts). VAL-6 rule: "Plugins SHOULD include a LICENSE file (and a SPDX license field) when distributing..." appliesTo "plugins intended for distribution" — git-guard is distributed via the marketplace, so the item applies. No conflictNote on VAL-6, and CLAUDE.md does not document an intentional "no per-plugin LICENSE" house rule, so this is not an excused deliberate choice.

The repo ROOT has a LICENSE (1.0K) which conventionally covers all plugins in a monorepo marketplace, and the SPDX license field requirement IS met in both plugin.json and README. So the distribution-license intent is largely satisfied; what remains is the SHOULD-level omission of a per-plugin LICENSE in the plugin dir itself. This is a documentation/convention omission, not a defect — claude plugins validate does not fail, no contract is broken, no manifest field is missing (SPDX present). Severity minor is correct; the auditor's calibration (SHOULD not MUST, root LICENSE noted, optional fix) is sound. Confirmed as a genuine minor gap.

verifiedGaps.evidence: plugins/shell-guard/.claude-plugin/plugin.json:3 version '0.3.0'; .claude-plugin/marketplace.json:9 sets metadata.version '0.2.0' but the shell-guard entry (:28-32) sets no per-plugin version

verification.note:
REFUTED — this is a pass, not a gap. MKT-11's hazard is version set in BOTH plugin.json AND the marketplace entry (appliesTo: "marketplace.json plugins[].version vs the referenced plugin.json version"); the plugin.json value then silently wins. Rule has no conflictNote.

Re-read of the files confirms the rule is SATISFIED, not violated:
- /Users/user/git/cc-goodies/plugins/shell-guard/.claude-plugin/plugin.json:3 → "version": "0.3.0" (the single source of truth).
- /Users/user/git/cc-goodies/.claude-plugin/marketplace.json: the shell-guard plugins[] entry (the object with "name": "shell-guard") declares NO version field. So plugins[].version is absent → there is no plugin.json-vs-entry duplication → the hazard cannot occur. This is exactly the intended pattern (define version once, in plugin.json).

The cited metadata.version "0.2.0" is the marketplace-level metadata field, an entirely different key from plugins[].version; MKT-11 says nothing about it, so comparing the two is a misapplication of the rule. The cited file contents are accurate (evidenceAccurate=true), but they demonstrate compliance, not a violation.

The auditor's own note concedes the gap is illusory ("no plugin.json-vs-entry version duplication — the rule's hazard is avoided", "Treated as effectively satisfied", "No fix needed"). Flagging a satisfied item as even a nit-severity gap is incorrect. Corrected verdict: pass; no severity.

Verb-first and specific, no 'command' filler. allowed-tools present per repo convention. Length is a documentation nit, not a load error; could trim if strict 60-char adherence is wanted.

verification.note:
Evidence is factually accurate but does not show a violation. Measured lengths: plugins/shell-guard/commands/shell-guard.md:2 description = 129 chars (claim said ~120); plugins/shell-guard/commands/shell-guard-uninstall.md:2 description = 165 chars by char-count incl. multibyte em-dash (claim said ~150). Both declare allowed-tools on line 3 (shell-guard.md: "Read, Write, Edit, Bash(...)"; uninstall: "Bash(rm:*), Bash(cp:*), ...Read"). Both descriptions are verb-first ("View or change", "Remove"), specific, no "command" filler.

The claimed violation hinges on a "~60-char guideline" that does NOT exist in this repo. CLAUDE.md's Commands section mandates only `description` + `allowed-tools` frontmatter whose body instructs Claude — no length cap. Empirically the house norm is the opposite: ALL 10 command descriptions across every plugin run 115–233 chars (git-guard 136/159, rtk-hook 146/231, project-scope 233, session-finalise 212, statusline 115/172). The two shell-guard descriptions (129/165) sit mid-range of that established, deliberate convention — they double as the skill-listing/tool-menu text (confirmed: they match the skill descriptions surfaced in the session skill list verbatim) and are written to be self-explanatory there. The auditor's own note concedes "Length is a documentation nit, not a load error" and that allowed-tools is "present per repo convention." There is no applicable rule being broken; this is a deliberate, consistent house-rule choice, so the correct verdict is pass, not gap. Refuting the claim.

verifiedGaps.evidence: commands/shell-guard-uninstall.md:1-4 frontmatter sets no disable-model-invocation; it deletes ~/.claude/shell-guard.conf (:23)
verifiedGaps.note: shell-guard-uninstall is destructive (rm of a config file) and the rubric flags such commands for disable-model-invocation:true. It is mitigated by in-body confirmation gates (:22 'with confirmation', :33 'ask before deleting'), but the frontmatter flag would stop autonomous model invocation. Adding disable-model-invocation:true to shell-guard-uninstall.md would close it. (/shell-guard config command is interactive but non-destructive; flag optional there.)

verification.note:
Verified and upheld. CMD-6 rule: "Set disable-model-invocation:true for commands that must be manually invoked, are destructive/irreversible, or require human judgment; use it sparingly." appliesTo explicitly names "*-uninstall control commands" — this is exactly that. No conflictNote field exists on the item.

Evidence is accurate: /Users/user/git/cc-goodies/plugins/shell-guard/commands/shell-guard-uninstall.md frontmatter (lines 1-4) sets only description + allowed-tools, no disable-model-invocation; line 20-22 backs up and `rm -f` deletes ~/.claude/shell-guard.conf. The command is genuinely destructive (file removal outside the plugin dir), so the item applies — not n-a.

Not a documented house-rule choice: grepped the whole repo; the string "disable-model-invocation" appears ONLY in rubric.json:498 (the rule itself). CLAUDE.md conventions never address the flag, so the omission is undocumented drift, not a deliberate, documented decision.

Severity minor is correct. In-body confirmation gates exist ("confirm" at :20-21, "ask before deleting" at :33) but those do not stop autonomous model invocation — the frontmatter flag does, which is the rule's point; this is a mitigation, not a cure. The gap is convention/best-practice drift (no contract break, no install/uninstall asymmetry, and the deleted conf IS its own revert so it is not durable-state-without-revert) → minor, not major/blocker. Fix: add disable-model-invocation:true to the frontmatter.

Context note (not affecting this item): the same omission spans all sibling mutating commands — git-guard-uninstall, rtk-hook-uninstall, statusline-uninstall, statusline-install all lack the flag too — so it is a marketplace-wide convention gap, but the cited shell-guard file does exhibit it as claimed.

verification.note:
Evidence verified and accurate. plugins/shell-guard/ ships no LICENSE file (confirmed via ls -la; in fact none of the 7 plugins do) and no per-plugin .gitignore. Root /Users/user/git/cc-goodies/LICENSE exists (1.0K). plugin.json:9 declares "license": "MIT" — SPDX field present and correct. shell-guard is distributed (marketplace.json source ./plugins/shell-guard), so VAL-6 applies — not n-a.

VAL-6 rule: "Plugins SHOULD include a LICENSE file (and a SPDX license field) when distributing, and a .gitignore when there are generated artifacts/local files to exclude." appliesTo: "plugins intended for distribution". The rubric item has NO conflictNote field (keys: appliesTo, dimension, id, rule, sources), and no global conflictNotes/notes in the rubric.

The SHOULD is substantially met: (a) SPDX license field present; (b) root LICENSE covers the whole marketplace per the standard monorepo single-LICENSE pattern; (c) the .gitignore sub-clause is fully satisfied — shell-guard ships no generated artifacts/local files (a root .gitignore for .DS_Store already exists), so no plugin-level .gitignore is needed. The only unmet sliver is a per-plugin LICENSE copy.

This is a consistent marketplace-wide convention (all 7 plugins identical), not a shell-guard-specific defect, and the literal "LICENSE file" wording of a SHOULD is missed only cosmetically. Real, applicable, but minor/cosmetic. Verdict stays gap at severity nit, matching the auditor's claim. Adding plugins/shell-guard/LICENSE would fully satisfy.

Reasoning to refute:

Its entire body (lines 6-11) just instructs Claude to invoke the session-finalise skill. allowed-tools constrains a command's own inline tool invocations; with none, the key has nothing to scope. Every command that DOES declare allowed-tools (git-guard, shell-guard, rtk-hook, statusline install/uninstall and config commands) is one that performs real inline operations (Write/Edit/rm/cp/jq). The split is principled: executing commands declare allowed-tools; skill-alias commands omit it. session-finalise follows that pattern exactly.

3. CLAUDE.md's allowed-tools language is tied to executing commands ("Anything a plugin can't do declaratively ... is done via a command that reads, shows the diff, confirms, and merges with jq"). It does not mandate allowed-tools on every command regardless of whether it executes anything.

Evidence verified accurate. /Users/user/git/cc-goodies/plugins/project-scope/commands/project-scope.md lines 1-4 declare only `description` and `argument-hint`; no `allowed-tools` key. CMD-3 rule: "Declare allowed-tools (an array) scoped as tightly as possible…"; appliesTo "slash commands that need particular tools"; conflictNote is null (no documented house-rule exception).

Convention is real and well established: all 8 other command files in the repo declare tightly-scoped allowed-tools arrays (e.g. git-guard.md, rtk-hook.md, statusline-install.md). So omitting it here is genuine convention drift, and CMD-3 applies (this is a slash command). Not a validator failure — allowed-tools is optional — so blocker/major are wrong; minor (convention drift / documentation omission) is correct. No durable external state, no broken contract.

One refuting nuance the auditor should note (does NOT overturn the gap, but tempers it): this is a thin ALIAS command. Its body executes nothing — it only instructs Claude to invoke the project-scope skill, and the Bash/Read/Edit/Write/AskUserQuestion tool use all happens inside the SKILL, which runs under its own session permissions (a command's allowed-tools does not gate a delegated skill). The sibling alias session-finalise.md omits allowed-tools for the identical reason, so there is a consistent, defensible pattern: alias commands that run no tools themselves don't strictly "need particular tools" per appliesTo. The cleanest resolution is the auditor's documented-exception option (note that alias commands intentionally omit allowed-tools because execution lives in the skill) rather than adding an array that wouldn't actually constrain the skill. Confirmed as a minor gap with that caveat.
